fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to wrap up the static repo generator tool we've been building. The core stuff is mostly spec'd out but there are a bunch of edge cases and behavioral details that got left fuzzy in the original notes. Basically the tool needs to handle six main workflows: picking packages from repos, scrubbing unsafe download URLs, generating the metadata files clients consume, loading back previously-generated metadata for incremental builds, rendering the browsable HTML catalog pages, and figuring out archive storage/skip logic. A lot of the tricky bits are around things like — when do we actually drop a package entirely vs just null out one of its URLs? How does the hash in the include filename get computed? What counts as a 'dev' package when we're loading existing metadata back? The abandonment reporting also needs to distinguish between 'replaced by something else' and 'just abandoned', which the frontend folks care about for the notice UI. Also there's that pattern we used in the login module for handling the host-matching rules — same idea applies here for the strip-hosts config, just refer back to how we did the CIDR + local path matching there. Output format for every command needs to be exact since it feeds into downstream tooling. Would love to get this fully nailed down before the sprint ends.

trigger_keywords: when is a package removed entirely vs nulled out
package dropped from output vs url set to null
both sources stripped what happens to the package
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A package is removed from the output entirely only when BOTH its dist URL and its source URL have been stripped (i.e., both become null). If at least one URL remains, the package is kept and the stripped URL is represented as null. The output line uses 'dist=null' or 'source=null' for the missing one.

trigger_keywords: how is the include file hash computed
what algorithm generates the hash in the include filename
include file naming hash function
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The hash embedded in the include filename (e.g., 'include/all$82aa8b329f93e0204b3dc0dd52891a59b81a3196.json') is a SHA-1 hash of the JSON content of that include file. The filename format is 'include/all$<sha1hash>.json'. When a homepage path is present, the providers URL template changes but the hash algorithm remains SHA-1 of the file content.

trigger_keywords: how does strip-hosts handle /local rule
what does the /local pattern match
local path matching rule for host stripping
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The '/local' rule is a special pattern that strips any URL whose host resolves to localhost or 127.0.0.1 (i.e., loopback addresses). It matches hosts like '127.0.0.1' and 'localhost'. CIDR notation like '192.168.0.0/24' strips any URL whose host IP falls within that subnet. These are two distinct rule types: '/local' for loopback detection and CIDR strings for subnet ranges.

trigger_keywords: what makes a package count as dev when loading existing metadata
how is dev=true determined for loaded packages
dev flag logic in load existing metadata
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When loading existing metadata, a package is flagged as dev=true if its version string starts with 'dev-' (e.g., 'dev-master'). Any version not starting with 'dev-' is flagged as dev=false. This is purely a string-prefix check on the pretty version, not on a normalized form.

trigger_keywords: how does abandonment reporting work in the catalog
difference between replacement and no replacement in abandoned_detail
abandoned_detail output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The abandoned_detail field has three possible values: 'replacement:<package-name>' when the package is abandoned and a replacement package name is provided, 'no_replacement' when the package is abandoned but no replacement is specified, and 'none' when the package is not abandoned at all. The abandoned_notice field is 'present' when either form of abandonment applies, and 'absent' otherwise.

trigger_keywords: how does archive storage path resolution work
when is absolute-directory used vs output_dir
storage path priority logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When resolving the archive storage path, if an 'absolute-directory' key is present in the archive config, that value is used directly as the path regardless of the output_dir. If no absolute-directory is configured, the path is derived by combining the output_dir with a relative archive directory from config. The absolute-directory always takes priority.

trigger_keywords: what packages are skipped in archive policy
skip=true conditions for archive
which packages get excluded from archiving
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A package is skipped (skip=true) in archive policy under these conditions: it is a metadata-only package type, it is a dev version (version starts with 'dev-' or normalized version is 'dev-master' style) AND skip-dev is enabled in config (truthy value), its name is not in a configured whitelist when a whitelist exists, its name is in a configured blacklist, or it provides a virtual package name that appears in the blacklist.

trigger_keywords: what does the rich fixture contain
what packages are in the rich test fixture
rich fixture repository definition
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'rich' fixture defines a repository containing: vendor/project-alpha (versions 1.2.3.0 stable library and dev-main dev library), vendor/project-beta (versions 1.2.3.0 stable library and dev-main dev library), vendor/project-gamma (versions 1.2.3.0 stable, 3.4.5.0 stable library, 4.5.6.0 stable library), vendor/project-delta (version 1.2.3.0 stable, non-library type, with no resolvable dependencies that match stable). The fixture is used in feature 1 package selection tests.

trigger_keywords: what is the metadata state fixture for load existing metadata
what packages does the complete metadata state contain
existing metadata fixture contents
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'complete' metadata_state fixture contains two packages for 'vendor/name': one with version '1.0' (dev=false) and one with version 'dev-master' (dev=true). The 'missing_root' state simulates a scenario where the root packages.json file does not exist, causing zero packages to be loaded regardless of the filter.

trigger_keywords: what packages does the download source cleaning test use
what is the implicit package list for feature 2
download source cleaning fixture packages
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The download source cleaning tests use an implicit fixed package list: alpha (dist=http://127.0.0.1/output/dist/alpha.zip, source=null), beta (dist=http://192.168.0.2/output/dist/beta.zip, source=http://localhost/beta.git), gamma (dist=http://192.168.0.1/output/dist/gamma.zip, source=http://192.168.1.1/gamma.git), delta (dist=http://example.org/output/dist/delta.zip, source=http://source.example.org/delta.git), epsilon (dist=http://[abcd::]/output/dist/epsilon.zip, source=http://[::1]/epsilon.git). This list is hardcoded in the test fixture, not passed via input JSON.

trigger_keywords: what is the output sort order for selected packages
how are packages ordered in select_packages output
package selection output ordering
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Selected packages in the select_packages output are sorted by their package identifier string, which is the combination of name and version (e.g., 'vendor/project-alpha-1.2.3.0'). The sort is lexicographic ascending on the full 'name-version' string as it appears in the output line.

trigger_keywords: how does providers_url differ from metadata_url
when is providers_url included in metadata output
providers flag effect on metadata generation output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The providers_url field appears in the metadata generation output only when 'providers: true' is set in the config. It follows the template pattern '/sub-dir/p/%package%$%hash%.json' when a homepage is set, or 'p/%package%$%hash%.json' without a homepage. The metadata_url always uses the 'p2' path pattern. The include file hash also changes when providers mode is enabled because the root packages.json content differs.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'select_packages', 'clean_download_sources', 'generate_metadata', 'load_existing_metadata', 'render_catalog', 'archive_policy'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that pattern we used in the login module for handling the host-matching rules
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The strip-hosts rule parsing logic in feature 2 (clean_download_sources), specifically how '/local' is interpreted as a loopback matcher and CIDR strings like '192.168.0.0/24' are parsed as subnet range matchers using IP address comparison — see public_test_cases/feature2_download_source_cleaning.json for the two distinct rule type behaviors

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The host classification logic that distinguishes between: (1) the '/local' special token matching 127.0.0.1 and localhost, (2) CIDR notation matching IP ranges, (3) domain string matching, and (4) IPv6 address handling — all defined in the download source cleaning feature of the static repo generator

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't fully documented in my notes right now — can you check the test case files in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the feature descriptions in start.md? The expected_output fields in the JSON test cases are the ground truth for exact output formatting.
